does: This paper investigates how video inputs can be weaponized to jailbreak multimodal large language models by introducing Multi-Clip Video (MCV) SafetyBench, a dataset of 2,920 videos designed to systematically evaluate MLLM vulnerabilities. The authors conduct experiments across eight representative video MLLMs and demonstrate that attack success consistently increases with the number of clips in a video, showing that videos are more vulnerable than static images, dynamic videos are more vulnerable than static ones, and videos containing diverse contexts related to harmful queries elicit higher jailbreak rates. The paper characterizes which properties of video inputs drive this vulnerability and proposes a defense mechanism that exploits the relative robustness of the image modality to mitigate these attacks.
connects: This work extends a growing body of research on adversarial vulnerabilities in multimodal systems, building on prior jailbreak studies that demonstrated visual inputs can bypass safety alignment in MLLMs. It responds to the gap between image-based jailbreak work and the emerging capabilities of video-processing MLLMs, directly addressing which input properties make video particularly dangerous. The research connects to broader work on adversarial robustness in vision-language models and safety alignment in large language models, while also drawing on research into how model confidence and input diversity affect vulnerability. By systematically studying the relationship between input composition and jailbreak success, the paper builds on methodologies from adversarial machine learning and safety evaluation benchmarking.
enables: This work enables more targeted defense strategies for video MLLMs by identifying specific vulnerabilities tied to input diversity and temporal composition. The findings point toward future research on hardening multimodal systems against sequential or compositional attacks, designing video safety filters that account for clip diversity, and developing more robust alignment techniques that maintain safety guarantees across modalities. The characterization of which properties drive vulnerability opens pathways for developing modality-specific safety mechanisms and understanding how temporal and compositional information interact with model reasoning. Results also suggest future work on cross-modal robustness, studying whether vulnerabilities in one modality can be mitigated by insights from another, and designing benchmarks that capture the full space of adversarial video constructions.
